---
title: "HL7 v2 — Message Model and Limitations"
lang: en-GB
---

## HL7 v2 and the Message Model {#sec-hl7v2-model}

### The problem HL7 v2 solved

Before HL7, each hospital information system operated as an isolated
silo. Exchanging clinical data between systems — for example, between
the admissions system and the laboratory — required manual intervention
or proprietary point-to-point solutions. **HL7 v2** was designed to
solve this problem: an application-layer protocol (OSI layer 7) for
the automatic exchange of clinical messages between heterogeneous
systems [@HL7_v28].

HL7 v2 achieves a degree of **semantic interoperability**: data flows
automatically between different systems with shared meaning. The unit
of exchange is a *message*, sent when a clinical event occurs. The
format is structured text, human-readable: segments separated by
special characters (*pipes* and *carets*). The standard is open,
maintained by the HL7 International consortium since 1987 [@HL7_v28].

The adoption was remarkable: more than 35 billion HL7 v2 messages are
estimated to be processed every year worldwide, and the protocol is
present in 95% of hospitals [@Bhagat2019]. Version 3 (2005) and FHIR
(2011) coexist with v2 — they have not replaced it.

### A standard with 35 years of longevity

HL7 v2 is deeply embedded in existing hospital systems (HIS, LIS, RIS,
PACS). Replacing it would require migrating decades of active interfaces.
@tbl-hl7-versions summarises the main versions.

| Year | Version | Milestone |
|------|---------|-----------|
| 1987 | v2.0 | Creation |
| 1990 | v2.1 | First widespread adoption |
| 1994 | v2.2 | Refinement |
| 1999 | v2.3.1 | Most widely installed |
| 2003 | v2.5 | Domain expansion |
| 2014 | v2.8 | Last published version |

: Main versions of HL7 v2 [@HL7_v28]. {#tbl-hl7-versions}

The longevity is not accidental: HL7 v2 aligns with real clinical
workflows, is human-readable text, and benefits from a mature ecosystem
of tools and consultants. Any clinical integration project in a hospital
setting directly involves HL7 v2 messages in production
[@BensonGrieve2021].

### The trigger-event model

In HL7 v2, messages are not sent continuously — they are created when
something happens in the real world. A **trigger event** is a clinical
occurrence that causes an HL7 message to be generated. Without an event
there is no message: the system always waits for something to happen
before communicating. The content of the message depends on the event
that triggered it [@Bhagat2019].

```{=html}
<!--
  Diagram: trigger-event model.
  Timeline: clinical event → message generation → send → receiver.
  Teal palette.
-->
<div style="overflow-x:auto; margin:1.5rem 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 680 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
     style="width:100%;max-width:680px;font-family:sans-serif;">
  <rect width="680" height="200" rx="10" fill="#f8fbfc"/>

  <!-- Title -->
  <text x="340" y="22" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#0a3d4a">Trigger-Event Model — HL7 v2</text>

  <!-- Block: Clinical Event -->
  <rect x="20" y="40" width="140" height="80" rx="8" fill="#0a3d4a"/>
  <text x="90" y="72" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Clinical</text>
  <text x="90" y="88" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Event</text>
  <text x="90" y="108" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#e2f4f3">e.g. admission</text>

  <!-- Arrow -->
  <line x1="162" y1="80" x2="218" y2="80" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="2"
        marker-end="url(#te1)"/>
  <text x="190" y="72" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#0d7c8c">triggers</text>

  <!-- Block: HL7 Message -->
  <rect x="220" y="40" width="140" height="80" rx="8" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <text x="290" y="72" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">HL7 v2</text>
  <text x="290" y="88" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Message</text>
  <text x="290" y="108" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#e2f4f3">ADT^A01</text>

  <!-- Arrow -->
  <line x1="362" y1="80" x2="418" y2="80" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="2"
        marker-end="url(#te2)"/>
  <text x="390" y="72" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#1ab3a6">sent to</text>

  <!-- Block: Receiving System -->
  <rect x="420" y="40" width="140" height="80" rx="8" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="490" y="72" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Receiving</text>
  <text x="490" y="88" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">System</text>
  <text x="490" y="108" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#0a3d4a">e.g. PACS, LIS, EMR</text>

  <!-- Examples -->
  <rect x="20" y="140" width="140" height="22" rx="4" fill="#e2f4f3"/>
  <text x="90" y="155" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#0a3d4a">Admission → ADT^A01</text>
  <rect x="170" y="140" width="140" height="22" rx="4" fill="#e2f4f3"/>
  <text x="240" y="155" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#0a3d4a">Order request → ORM^O01</text>
  <rect x="320" y="140" width="140" height="22" rx="4" fill="#e2f4f3"/>
  <text x="390" y="155" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#0a3d4a">Lab result → ORU^R01</text>
  <rect x="470" y="140" width="140" height="22" rx="4" fill="#e2f4f3"/>
  <text x="540" y="155" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#0a3d4a">Discharge → ADT^A03</text>

  <text x="340" y="188" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" fill="#64748b">
    Figure 6.1 — Trigger-event model: clinical event → HL7 message → receiving system
  </text>

  <defs>
    <marker id="te1" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#0d7c8c"/></marker>
    <marker id="te2" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#1ab3a6"/></marker>
  </defs>
</svg>
</div>
```

### Message types and trigger codes

Each HL7 message is identified by two codes joined by a caret (`^`): the
message type and the trigger event. The type is a three-letter uppercase
acronym (e.g. `ADT`); the trigger is a letter followed by two digits
(e.g. `A01`). @tbl-msg-types lists the most relevant types in a hospital
context.

| Type | Full name | Trigger | Clinical event |
|------|-----------|---------|----------------|
| ADT | Patient Administration | A01 | Patient admission |
| ADT | Patient Administration | A02 | Patient transfer |
| ADT | Patient Administration | A03 | Patient discharge |
| ADT | Patient Administration | A04 | Outpatient / emergency registration |
| ORM | Order Message | O01 | Lab or exam order |
| ORU | Observation Result | R01 | Lab result |
| MDM | Medical Document Management | T02 | Clinical document creation |
| DFT | Detail Financial Transaction | P03 | Financial / billing transaction |

: Message types and main triggers in HL7 v2 [@HL7_v28; @Bhagat2019]. {#tbl-msg-types}

`ADT^A01` means: *Patient Administration* (type) + *Admit* (trigger).
The two codes together uniquely identify the message in any conformant
implementation.

## Structure of an HL7 v2 Message {#sec-hl7v2-structure}

### Five-level hierarchy

An HL7 v2 message is composed of five levels of granularity — from the
broadest to the most specific [@HL7_v28]:

1. **Message** — the complete unit of exchange (e.g. `ADT^A01`)
2. **Segment** — a logical grouping of related fields (e.g. `MSH`, `PID`, `PV1`)
3. **Field** — a data unit within a segment (e.g. `PID-5` = patient name)
4. **Component** — a subdivision of a field, separated by `^` (e.g. family name `^` given name)
5. **Subcomponent** — a subdivision of a component, separated by `&` (e.g. code `&` institution)

```{=html}
<!--
  Diagram: HL7 v2 five-level hierarchy.
-->
<div style="overflow-x:auto; margin:1.5rem 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 660 320" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
     style="width:100%;max-width:660px;font-family:sans-serif;">
  <rect width="660" height="320" rx="10" fill="#f8fbfc"/>

  <!-- Level 1: Message -->
  <rect x="20" y="20" width="620" height="48" rx="8" fill="#0a3d4a"/>
  <text x="140" y="49" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Message</text>
  <text x="340" y="49" font-size="11" fill="#e2f4f3">ADT^A01 — admission message</text>

  <line x1="330" y1="68" x2="330" y2="88" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="2"
        marker-end="url(#h1)"/>

  <!-- Level 2: Segment -->
  <rect x="50" y="90" width="560" height="48" rx="8" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <text x="150" y="119" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Segment</text>
  <text x="330" y="119" font-size="11" fill="#e2f4f3">MSH · EVN · PID · NK1 · PV1</text>

  <line x1="330" y1="138" x2="330" y2="158" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="2"
        marker-end="url(#h2)"/>

  <!-- Level 3: Field -->
  <rect x="80" y="160" width="500" height="48" rx="8" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="160" y="189" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Field</text>
  <text x="330" y="189" font-size="11" fill="#0a3d4a">PID-5:  BAPTISTA^JOAO^A^JUNIOR</text>

  <line x1="330" y1="208" x2="330" y2="228" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="2"
        marker-end="url(#h1)"/>

  <!-- Level 4: Component -->
  <rect x="110" y="230" width="440" height="48" rx="8" fill="#e2f4f3" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="180" y="259" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#0a3d4a">Component</text>
  <text x="330" y="259" font-size="11" fill="#0d7c8c">BAPTISTA · JOAO · A · JUNIOR  (separated by ^)</text>

  <line x1="330" y1="278" x2="330" y2="293" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.5"
        marker-end="url(#h2)"/>

  <!-- Level 5: Subcomponent -->
  <rect x="140" y="295" width="380" height="14" rx="4" fill="#e2f4f3" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="330" y="306" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#0a3d4a">Subcomponent — separated by &amp;  (e.g. code &amp; issuing institution)</text>

  <defs>
    <marker id="h1" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#0d7c8c"/></marker>
    <marker id="h2" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#1ab3a6"/></marker>
  </defs>
</svg>
</div>
```

### The delimiters

Five special characters define the grammar of HL7 v2. They are declared
in fields `MSH-1` and `MSH-2` of every message, meaning a parser can
verify their presence before processing any other field [@HL7_v28].

| Character | Name | Function | Example |
|-----------|------|----------|---------|
| `&#124;` | Pipe | Field separator | `PID&#124;1&#124;&#124;PAT001&#124;&#124;SILVA^ANA` |
| `^` | Caret | Component separator | `SILVA^ANA^M` → family name ^ given name ^ initial |
| `~` | Tilde | Repetition separator | `PID-3: ID001~ID002` → two alternative IDs |
| `\` | Backslash | Escape character | `\F\` represents a literal pipe in content |
| `&` | Ampersand | Subcomponent separator | `ID001&UTAD` → ID ^ issuing institution |

: HL7 v2 delimiters — declared in MSH-1 and MSH-2 [@HL7_v28]. {#tbl-delimiters}

`MSH-1` always contains the pipe — it is the field separator itself.
`MSH-2` contains the other four in order `^~\&`. These two fields are
the first in any message and allow the receiver to determine the
delimiters before beginning to parse.

### Segments: the logical unit

A **segment** is a logical grouping of related fields. It begins with a
three-letter uppercase identifier followed by a pipe. The position
determines the meaning of the field — `PID-5` is always the patient name,
regardless of the implementation. @tbl-segments describes the essential
segments in an ADT context.

| ID | Name | Presence | Function |
|----|------|----------|---------|
| MSH | Message Header | Mandatory, unique | Sender, receiver, message type, version |
| EVN | Event Type | Mandatory | Event type and occurrence timestamp |
| PID | Patient Identification | Mandatory | Name, ID, date of birth, gender, address |
| PV1 | Patient Visit | Mand./Optional | Ward, bed, attending physician, service |
| NK1 | Next of Kin | Optional, repeatable | Family members or emergency contacts |
| NTE | Notes and Comments | Optional, repeatable | Free-text notes |
| ERR | Error | Optional | Error detail in negative ACK messages |

: Essential segments in ADT messages [@HL7_v28]. {#tbl-segments}

The presence notation follows a normalised convention: no brackets means
mandatory; `[ ]` means optional; `{ }` means repeatable; `[{ }]` means
optional and repeatable.

### Fields: positional and fill states

Within a segment, fields are **positional**: the position number defines
the meaning. A parser counts the pipes to know which field it is in.
Trailing empty fields may be omitted — the parser infers their absence.

A field can be in three distinct states with different semantics
[@HL7_v28]:

- **Populated** — the sending system provides a value. Example: `19610615`
- **Not Populated** — the field exists but is empty; the sender does not
  have the value. Example: `||`
- **Null** — an explicit instruction for the receiver to delete the value
  it holds. Example: `|""|`

The distinction between *Not Populated* and *Null* is critical: confusing
the two can cause silent data corruption in the receiving system. *Not
Populated* means "I don't have the value"; *Null* means "delete the value
you have".

## Essential Segments {#sec-hl7v2-segments}

### MSH — Message header

The MSH is always the first segment and defines the full context of the
message. @tbl-msh describes the most relevant fields, using the example
from the lectures:

```
MSH|^~\&|ADT1|ULSTMAD|SCLINICO|ULSTMAD|200707181123||ADT^A01^ADT_A01|MSG00001|P|2.8
```

| Field | Value | Name | Meaning |
|-------|-------|------|---------|
| MSH-1 | `&#124;` | Field Separator | The pipe itself — the only field whose value is the delimiter |
| MSH-2 | `^~\&` | Encoding Characters | The four secondary delimiters |
| MSH-3 | `ADT1` | Sending Application | Sending application |
| MSH-4 | `ULSTMAD` | Sending Facility | Sending institution |
| MSH-5 | `SCLINICO` | Receiving Application | Receiving application |
| MSH-6 | `ULSTMAD` | Receiving Facility | Receiving institution |
| MSH-7 | `200707181123` | Date/Time | Timestamp: YYYYMMDDHHII |
| MSH-9 | `ADT^A01^ADT_A01` | Message Type | Type ^ Trigger ^ structure |
| MSH-10 | `MSG00001` | Message Control ID | Unique ID — returned by receiver in ACK |
| MSH-11 | `P` | Processing ID | P = production · T = test · D = debug |
| MSH-12 | `2.8` | Version ID | HL7 version used |

: Main fields of the MSH segment [@HL7_v28]. {#tbl-msh}

`MSH-10` (*Message Control ID*) is the field the receiver returns in
the ACK message to confirm which message was received — it is the
correlation mechanism between request and response.

### EVN — Event type

The EVN segment records the event type and the relevant timestamps.
It appears immediately after the MSH:

```
EVN|A01|200707181123||
```

The most relevant fields are: `EVN-1` (trigger code, e.g. `A01`),
`EVN-2` (moment the event was recorded in the system) and `EVN-5`
(identifier of the operator who recorded the event). `EVN-3` (planned
date) and `EVN-4` (event reason) are optional and frequently empty.

In a transfer message (`ADT^A02`), the difference between `EVN-2`
(recording timestamp) and `EVN-6` (actual event time) documents the
interval between the clinical occurrence and its administrative
registration.

### PID — Patient identification

The PID segment uniquely identifies the patient. Dissecting the example
from the lectures illustrates field positional semantics [@HL7_v28]:

```
PID|1||PATID1234^^^ULSTMAD^MR~123456789^^^PORTUGAL^SS||BAPTISTA^JOAO^A^JUNIOR
||19610615|M|||45^RUADOCARMO^^VILAREAL^VILAREAL^5000-678|||92212345
||S||PATID12345001^2^M10^ADT1^AN^A|
```

| Field | Value | Meaning |
|-------|-------|---------|
| PID-1 | `1` | Set ID — segment order number |
| PID-3 | `PATID1234^^^ULSTMAD^MR~123456789^^^PORTUGAL^SS` | Two IDs: internal MR + Social Security number |
| PID-5 | `BAPTISTA^JOAO^A^JUNIOR` | Name: family name ^ given name ^ initial ^ suffix |
| PID-7 | `19610615` | Date of birth — 15 Jun 1961 |
| PID-8 | `M` | Gender — M (Male) |
| PID-11 | `45^RUADOCARMO^^VILAREAL^...^5000-678` | Structured address: street ^ ^ city ^ … ^ postal code |

: Main fields of the PID segment, João Baptista example [@HL7_v28]. {#tbl-pid}

Field `PID-3` illustrates repetition with `~`: two groups separated by
tilde correspond to two distinct identifiers for the same patient — the
hospital's internal ID and the Social Security number. Within each group,
`^` separates the identifier components: value, check digit, verification
code, assigning authority, type and authority name.

### PV1 — Patient visit

The PV1 segment describes the current visit: physical location,
attending physician and clinical service. Field `PV1-3` uses a
three-component structure identifying the location:
`ward^room^bed`.

```
PV1|1|I|CIR-10^64^01||||124340^SILVA^ANTONIO|||SUR||||ADM
```

`PV1-2` indicates the visit type (`I` = inpatient; `O` = outpatient;
`E` = emergency). `PV1-7` identifies the attending physician as
`code^family name^given name`. `PV1-10` indicates the clinical service
(`SUR` = surgery, `ICU` = intensive care unit). In a discharge message,
`PV1-44` and `PV1-45` record the admission date and discharge date
respectively, allowing any receiving system to calculate the length of
stay.

## Message Confirmation: the ACK {#sec-hl7v2-ack}

After receiving a message, the destination system sends an **ACK**
message to confirm receipt or to reject with error detail. The ACK
always includes an `MSA` segment whose field `MSA-2` repeats the
`MSH-10` of the original message, ensuring correlation between request
and response [@HL7_v28].

The response codes in field `MSA-1` are:

- **AA** (*Application Accept*) — message processed successfully
- **AE** (*Application Error*) — processed but with a content error
- **AR** (*Application Reject*) — rejected; processing was not possible

```{=html}
<!--
  Diagram: message exchange + ACK.
-->
<div style="overflow-x:auto; margin:1.5rem 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 640 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
     style="width:100%;max-width:640px;font-family:sans-serif;">
  <rect width="640" height="240" rx="10" fill="#f8fbfc"/>

  <!-- System A -->
  <rect x="20" y="20" width="120" height="200" rx="8" fill="#0a3d4a" opacity="0.08"
        stroke="#0a3d4a" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="20" y="20" width="120" height="32" rx="8" fill="#0a3d4a"/>
  <text x="80" y="41" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">System A</text>
  <text x="80" y="58" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#0a3d4a">(Sender)</text>

  <!-- System B -->
  <rect x="500" y="20" width="120" height="200" rx="8" fill="#0d7c8c" opacity="0.08"
        stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="500" y="20" width="120" height="32" rx="8" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <text x="560" y="41" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">System B</text>
  <text x="560" y="58" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#0d7c8c">(Receiver)</text>

  <!-- Arrow: message A→B -->
  <line x1="142" y1="100" x2="498" y2="100" stroke="#0a3d4a" stroke-width="2"
        marker-end="url(#ack1)"/>
  <text x="320" y="90" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#0a3d4a">ADT^A01  (MSH-10: MSG00001)</text>

  <!-- Arrow: ACK B→A -->
  <line x1="498" y1="140" x2="142" y2="140" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="2"
        stroke-dasharray="6" marker-end="url(#ack2)"/>
  <text x="320" y="130" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#1ab3a6">ACK  (MSA-1: AA · MSA-2: MSG00001)</text>

  <!-- Response codes -->
  <rect x="180" y="160" width="280" height="50" rx="6" fill="#e2f4f3" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="320" y="177" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#0a3d4a">MSA-1:</text>
  <text x="230" y="195" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#16a34a">AA = Accepted</text>
  <text x="320" y="195" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">AE = Error</text>
  <text x="410" y="195" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">AR = Rejected</text>

  <text x="320" y="225" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" fill="#64748b">
    Figure 6.2 — Message exchange and ACK: MSA-2 correlates request with response (HL7 v2.8, §2)
  </text>

  <defs>
    <marker id="ack1" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#0a3d4a"/></marker>
    <marker id="ack2" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#1ab3a6"/></marker>
  </defs>
</svg>
</div>
```

In a negative ACK (`AE`), the `ERR` segment provides error detail:
field `ERR-2` identifies the segment and field position with the error
(`PID^5` = field 5 of PID, patient name); `ERR-3` contains the error
code; `ERR-4` indicates severity (`E` = error, `W` = warning). This
mechanism allows the sender to diagnose exactly what failed.

## ADT Messages: the Patient's Administrative Journey {#sec-hl7v2-adt}

**ADT** (*Admission, Discharge, Transfer*) messages track each
administrative state change of the patient. Whenever a patient is
admitted, changes service, or is discharged, an ADT message is generated
and distributed to all subscribed systems. They are the most frequent
messages in any hospital [@HL7_v28].

To illustrate the three central events, the lectures use a complete
clinical case: João A. Baptista Júnior, admitted to ULSTMAD on 18 July
2007 for surgery, transferred to ICU early the following morning, and
discharged on 25 July.

### ADT^A01 — Admission

The `A01` event signals that a patient has been admitted and assigned
a bed. It is the start of the hospital process: the HIS generates the
`ADT^A01` and distributes it to all subscribed systems [@HL7_v28].

In the case study, on 18 July 2007 at 11:23, João Baptista is admitted
to the surgery service (SUR), Ward CIR-10, Room 64, Bed 01, with
Dr. António Silva (#124340) as attending physician. The complete
message is:

```
MSH|^~\&|ADT1|ULSTMAD|SCLINICO|ULSTMAD|200707181123||ADT^A01^ADT_A01|MSG00001|P|2.8
EVN|A01|200707181123||
PID|1||PATID1234^^^ULSTMAD^MR~123456789^^^PORTUGAL^SS||BAPTISTA^JOAO^A^JUNIOR
||19610615|M|||45^RUADOCARMO^^VILAREAL^VILAREAL^5000-678|||92212345
||S||PATID12345001^2^M10^ADT1^AN^A|
NK1|1|PEREIRA^NILZA^F|SPO^SPOUSE||||NK^NEXT OF KIN
PV1|1|I|CIR-10^64^01||||124340^SILVA^ANTONIO|||SUR||||ADM
```

The message has five segments: MSH (envelope), EVN (event timestamp),
PID (patient identification, three lines), NK1 (spouse Nilza F. Pereira)
and PV1 (location and physician). `PV1-3` uses the structure
`CIR-10^64^01` — ward ^ room ^ bed.

### ADT^A02 — Transfer

The `A02` event is generated when an admitted patient changes location
within the hospital. The PID remains unchanged; the PV1 is updated with
the new location, new responsible physician and new service [@HL7_v28].

In the early hours of 19 July, João Baptista's condition worsens due
to a post-operative complication. He is transferred to the ICU at 02:30;
the event is recorded at 02:45:

```
MSH|^~\&|ADT1|ULSTMAD|SCLINICO|ULSTMAD|200707190245||ADT^A02^ADT_A02|000002|P|2.8||||
EVN|A02|200707190245||01||200707190230
PID|1||PATID1234^^^ULSTMAD^MR~123456789^^^PORTUGAL^SS||BAPTISTA^JOAO^A^JUNIOR
||19610615|M|||45^RUADOCARMO^^VILAREAL^VILAREAL^5000-678|GL||(259)123-456
||S||PATID12345001^2^M10^ADT1^AN^A|
PV1||I|UCI^01^02^ULSTMAD|||CIR-10^64^01|124340^SILVA^ANTONIO|856321^FERREIRA^CARLOS||ICU
|||||||856321^FERREIRA^CARLOS|S|||||||||||||||||||ULSTMAD|||||200707181123|
```

`EVN-2` (`200707190245`) records when the event was entered into the
system; `EVN-6` (`200707190230`) records the actual transfer time. The
15-minute gap documents the administrative delay. In PV1: `PV1-3` = new
location (`UCI^01^02`), `PV1-6` = previous location (`CIR-10^64^01`),
`PV1-8` = Dr. Ferreira (new responsible), `PV1-10` = ICU service.

### ADT^A03 — Discharge

The `A03` event records the end of the hospital stay. Field `PV1-36`
indicates the discharge destination; fields `PV1-44` and `PV1-45`
record the admission date and discharge date respectively [@HL7_v28].

On 25 July 2007 at 10:00, João Baptista is discharged home with home
health care (HH):

```
MSH|^~\&|ADT1|ULSTMAD|SCLINICO|ULSTMAD|200707251000||ADT^A03^ADT_A03|000003|P|2.8||||
EVN|A03|200707251000||01||200707251000
PID|1||PATID1234^^^ULSTMAD^MR~123456789^^^PORTUGAL^SS||BAPTISTA^JOAO^A^JUNIOR
||19610615|M|||45^RUADOCARMO^^VILAREAL^VILAREAL^5000-678|GL||(259)123-456
||S||PATID12345001^2^M10^ADT1^AN^A|
PV1||I|CIR-10^64^01||||124340^SILVA^ANTONIO|||SUR|||||||124340^SILVA^ANTONIO|S|1400|A
||||||||||||||||||HH|||ULSTMAD|||||200707181123|200707251000
```

`PV1-44` (`200707181123`) − `PV1-45` (`200707251000`) = 7-day hospital
stay, automatically calculable by any receiving system. Possible values
in `PV1-36` include: `HH` (home health care), `SNF` (skilled nursing
facility), `HSP` (transfer to another hospital) and `EXP` (expired).

```{=html}
<!--
  Diagram: ADT sequence A01 → A02 → A03 along a timeline.
-->
<div style="overflow-x:auto; margin:1.5rem 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 680 160" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
     style="width:100%;max-width:680px;font-family:sans-serif;">
  <rect width="680" height="160" rx="10" fill="#f8fbfc"/>

  <!-- Timeline -->
  <line x1="60" y1="80" x2="620" y2="80" stroke="#cbd5e1" stroke-width="2"/>

  <!-- Event 1: A01 -->
  <circle cx="140" cy="80" r="18" fill="#0a3d4a"/>
  <text x="140" y="85" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">A01</text>
  <text x="140" y="120" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#0a3d4a">Admission</text>
  <text x="140" y="134" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#64748b">18 Jul 11:23</text>
  <text x="140" y="148" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#64748b">CIR-10 · Dr. Silva</text>

  <!-- Event 2: A02 -->
  <circle cx="340" cy="80" r="18" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <text x="340" y="85" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">A02</text>
  <text x="340" y="120" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#0d7c8c">Transfer</text>
  <text x="340" y="134" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#64748b">19 Jul 02:30</text>
  <text x="340" y="148" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#64748b">ICU · Dr. Ferreira</text>

  <!-- Event 3: A03 -->
  <circle cx="560" cy="80" r="18" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="560" y="85" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">A03</text>
  <text x="560" y="120" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#1ab3a6">Discharge</text>
  <text x="560" y="134" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#64748b">25 Jul 10:00</text>
  <text x="560" y="148" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#64748b">Destination: HH</text>

  <!-- Duration -->
  <line x1="140" y1="55" x2="560" y2="55" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.5"
        stroke-dasharray="4" marker-start="url(#d1)" marker-end="url(#d2)"/>
  <text x="350" y="46" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#1ab3a6">7-day hospital stay</text>

  <defs>
    <marker id="d1" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="1" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M6,0 L0,3 L6,6 Z" fill="#1ab3a6"/></marker>
    <marker id="d2" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#1ab3a6"/></marker>
  </defs>
</svg>
</div>
```

Figure 6.3 — ADT sequence for the João Baptista case: admission (A01), transfer (A02) and discharge (A03).

## HL7 v2 Limitations {#sec-hl7v2-limitations}

### Structural fragility: no formal schema

HL7 v2 has no formal schema. Any string is accepted in any field — the
standard does not define automatic format validation. Field `PID-7`
(date of birth) should contain `YYYYMMDD`, but the receiver must accept
whatever arrives. Format errors pass silently until they surface in a
wrong clinical report [@HL7_v28].

```
PID|1||PAT001||SILVA^ANA||19850312|F|...    ← YYYYMMDD — correct
PID|1||PAT001||SILVA^ANA||1985-03-12|F|...  ← accepted, but different format
PID|1||PAT001||SILVA^ANA||12/03/1985|F|...  ← accepted, yet another dialect
```

A database with a `DATE` column automatically rejects `15/06/1961`. In
HL7 v2, no such mechanism exists. Each sending system has its own dialect
for dates, genders and codes; the integration engine accumulates
normalisation rules per system. As the hospital network grows, this
normalisation work becomes a permanent ongoing cost.

### Weak semantics: local value tables

HL7 v2 defines fields by name and position, but does not define the
vocabulary of their values. The specification provides **user-defined
tables**: each hospital chooses its own codes. What should be a shared
vocabulary becomes a private dialect of each institution
[@BensonGrieve2021].

The most common example is `PV1-4` (admission type):

| Hospital | Value in PV1-4 | Local meaning |
|----------|----------------|---------------|
| Hospital A | `U` | Urgência (Emergency) |
| Hospital B | `E` | Emergency |
| Hospital C | `1` | Urgent |

: Weak semantics: the same field, incompatible values across hospitals [@BensonGrieve2021]. {#tbl-semantics}

All three hospitals are conformant with the specification — and are
incompatible with each other. The same problem exists in `PID-8`
(gender), clinical service, diagnosis type and marital status: every
field with a *user-defined table* is a potential point of
incompatibility. Two "HL7 v2 conformant" systems may have completely
different semantics and require bilateral mapping before any data
exchange.

### Z-segments: private extensions

Any segment whose identifier begins with `Z` is reserved for local use.
The specification prohibits Z-segments in the normalised standard — they
are intentionally private and opaque. Each hospital creates its own
extra fields (e.g. `ZPI` for insurance information, `ZIN` for health
subsystem data) without publishing them [@HL7_v28].

The problem becomes apparent when integrating two hospitals:

```
Hospital A:  ZIN|ADSE|GROUP001|PLANO_B
Hospital B:  ZSG|ADSE|PLANO_B|GROUP001
```

The same content, different identifiers, different field order —
mutually incompatible. An external parser does not know what `ZIN` or
`ZSG` mean; it must be configured case by case, by bilateral agreement.
Z-segments are the most visible example of a broader problem: the
standard is open enough to generate incompatibilities even between
conformant implementations.

### Parsing: hidden complexity

Processing an HL7 v2 message is more demanding than simply splitting
strings on the pipe character. Three main sources of complexity can be
identified [@Bhagat2019]:

- **Repeatable fields with `~`** — a field may have multiple values
  separated by tilde; `PID-3` can contain several identifiers for the
  same patient; the parser must handle lists, not single values.
- **Opaque Z-segments** — a generic parser does not know what `ZIN`,
  `ZPI` or `ZCU` mean; it must be configured case by case.
- **Version differences** — the same field may have different structures
  between v2.3 and v2.8; in a real environment, different systems send
  different versions and the parser must handle all of them.

There is no universal parser for HL7 v2: every environment requires
system-specific and version-specific rules.

### The integration engine: the required piece

The limitations described above converge on one point: any real HL7 v2
environment requires an **integration middleware** — commonly called an
*integration engine*. Products such as Mirth Connect, Rhapsody or
InterSystems Ensemble receive messages from multiple systems, normalise
formats, map local tables, translate Z-segments and route to the correct
destinations [@BensonGrieve2021].

```{=html}
<!--
  Diagram: integration engine — N sending systems → engine → N receiving systems.
-->
<div style="overflow-x:auto; margin:1.5rem 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 680 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
     style="width:100%;max-width:680px;font-family:sans-serif;">
  <rect width="680" height="240" rx="10" fill="#f8fbfc"/>

  <!-- Sending systems (left) -->
  <rect x="15" y="30"  width="110" height="32" rx="6" fill="#0a3d4a"/>
  <text x="70" y="51" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">HIS (Admissions)</text>
  <rect x="15" y="80"  width="110" height="32" rx="6" fill="#0a3d4a"/>
  <text x="70" y="101" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">LIS (Laboratory)</text>
  <rect x="15" y="130" width="110" height="32" rx="6" fill="#0a3d4a"/>
  <text x="70" y="151" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">RIS (Radiology)</text>

  <!-- Arrows → engine -->
  <line x1="127" y1="46"  x2="245" y2="100" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#ie1)"/>
  <line x1="127" y1="96"  x2="245" y2="110" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#ie1)"/>
  <line x1="127" y1="146" x2="245" y2="120" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#ie1)"/>

  <!-- Integration Engine -->
  <rect x="247" y="70" width="186" height="80" rx="10" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <text x="340" y="98"  text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Integration Engine</text>
  <text x="340" y="115" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#e2f4f3">Mirth · Rhapsody · Ensemble</text>
  <text x="340" y="130" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#e2f4f3">Normalises · Maps · Routes</text>

  <!-- Arrows engine → receivers -->
  <line x1="435" y1="100" x2="548" y2="50"  stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#ie2)"/>
  <line x1="435" y1="110" x2="548" y2="100" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#ie2)"/>
  <line x1="435" y1="120" x2="548" y2="150" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#ie2)"/>

  <!-- Receiving systems (right) -->
  <rect x="550" y="30"  width="110" height="32" rx="6" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="605" y="51" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">EMR (Clinical)</text>
  <rect x="550" y="82"  width="110" height="32" rx="6" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="605" y="103" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Pharmacy</text>
  <rect x="550" y="134" width="110" height="32" rx="6" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="605" y="155" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">PACS (Imaging)</text>

  <text x="340" y="225" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" fill="#64748b">
    Figure 6.4 — Integration engine: the required piece in any real HL7 v2 environment
  </text>

  <defs>
    <marker id="ie1" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#0d7c8c"/></marker>
    <marker id="ie2" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#1ab3a6"/></marker>
  </defs>
</svg>
</div>
```

The integration engine is not optional: it is the piece that makes HL7
v2 work in heterogeneous environments. The cost the standard does not
show is the maintenance cost of this middleware: each new system joining
the network requires analysis, mapping and testing. As N systems grow,
the number of interfaces — and the cost of maintaining them — grows with
it. This is the structural limit of the bilateral model that HL7 v2
imposes.

### Balance: what HL7 v2 solved and where it fell short

HL7 v2 solved a real problem: it eliminated the manual exchange of data
between hospital systems, created a trigger-event model aligned with
clinical workflows, and survived 35 years of technological change. Its
limits only became apparent as scale grew.

| Aspect | Assessment |
|--------|------------|
| Elimination of manual data exchange | Solved |
| Trigger-event model aligned with clinical workflows | Solved |
| Global adoption (95% of hospitals) | Solved |
| Mature ecosystem of tools | Solved |
| Formal schema with automatic validation | Not solved |
| Shared semantics across institutions | Not solved |
| Interoperable private extensions (Z-segments) | Not solved |
| Universal parser | Not solved |
| Integration cost proportional to number of systems | Not solved |

: Balance of HL7 v2 [@HL7_v28; @BensonGrieve2021]. {#tbl-balance}

## Summary and Exercises {#sec-hl7v2-summary}

### Consolidation exercises {.unnumbered}

1. A laboratory system (LIS) receives an order sent by the prescribing
   system. Identify: (a) the HL7 message type used; (b) the trigger
   event; (c) the MSH fields that allow the LIS to determine who sent
   the order and which HL7 version it conforms to.

2. The `PID-3` field of a message contains the value
   `PATID1234^^^ULSTMAD^MR~123456789^^^PORTUGAL^SS`.
   Explain the role of the `~` character in this context and identify
   the two identifiers present, stating the type of each.

3. A negative ACK (AE) contains `ERR||PID^5|101^Required field missing|E`.
   Interpret each component of this segment and explain the practical
   consequence for the sending system.

4. Two hospitals use different values in `PV1-4` to classify emergency
   admissions: Hospital X uses `U`, Hospital Y uses `E`. Both claim
   to be HL7 v2 conformant. Explain how this situation is possible and
   what architectural component is needed to integrate them.

5. A hospital needs to transmit health insurance data (subsystem code,
   group number, plan) between the HIS and the pharmacy. The HL7 v2
   specification defines no standard segment for this purpose. What
   mechanism does the standard provide? What are the implications for
   interoperability with other hospitals?
